advisory_content: Some developed varieties of Blackgram include TARM 1, TARM 2, Jyoti , Kamdev (OUM 11-5), Pusa Bold 1, Dhauli, Pragyan, Durga (OBGG-52), Shreya (IPM 02-14), Meha (IPM 99-125), LGG 460, TU 40.

Farmers are advised to start land preparation immediately after the paddy harvest especially when grown under residual soil moisture after the harvest. Plough the field 2–3 times to break clods and obtain a fine tilth. Level the land properly to conserve residual moisture and ensure uniform germination. In areas with poor drainage, make broad beds and furrows or raised beds to avoid waterlogging. Apply well-decomposed FYM or compost @ 2 t/acre during the last ploughing to improve soil fertility and moisture-holding capacity. In case of dry soil, apply one pre-sowing irrigation to ensure uniform germination. Avoid deep sowing and ensure proper seed-soil contact. If soil moisture is inadequate, delay sowing until light rainfall or irrigation is available. Farmers should ensure proper field bunding to retain moisture and prevent runoff. In areas prone to moisture stress, apply light irrigation at flowering and pod formation stages, which are critical for yield improvement. Protect seedlings from pests like cutworm, termites.

Treat 10 kg of seeds are required for sowing in an acre area. Seed treatment should be done before 1 week of sowing with carbendazim @ 2 g/ kg or Carboxin 37.5 % + Thiram 37.5% D.S @ 2 -gram/ kg of seeds. On the day of sowing prepare a slurry of 20 g bacteria culture with jaggery @ 50 gram and 40 ml water for 1 kg seed and mix it with seeds, then shade dry and sow it in afternoon. Apply Trichoderma viride @ 4g / kg of seed or Pseudomonas fluorescens @ 10 g/kg seed. To increase the biological nitrogen fixation mix Rhizobium 200 g and PSB 250 g in 600 ml water per 10 kg of seed followed by drying for 12 hrs. in shade. Seeds are treated with Thiamethoxam 30% FS @ 10 ml/ kg To prevent sucking pest and with carbosulfan 25 EC @ 2g/ Kg of seed to prevent nematode infestation. Sow the seeds at 20 cm x 10 cm spacing after seed treatment is done.
Plough the field till fine tilth and apply 4 kg urea, 35 kg DAP, 13 kg MOP along with 2 tons well decomposed cowdung manure. Sow the seeds at 25 c.m x 10 c.m spacing after seed treatment is done.
To manage weeds at early stages in these crops, spray pre-emergence herbicide Pendimethalin 30 % E.C @ 1 litre /acre at 1-2 DAS by mixing in 200-litre of water.

Always use flat-fan or flood-jet nozzle and clean water for herbicide spraying.

advisory_content: During the flowering stage (30–45 DAS), Blackgram is highly sensitive to moisture stress, so maintaining adequate soil moisture is crucial. If there is no rainfall for 7–10 days, provide light irrigation (4–5 cm depth) using the furrow or check basin method, avoiding water stagnation. Ensure proper drainage to prevent diseases like powdery mildew and rust. Irrigate in the early morning or late afternoon to reduce evaporation losses. Mulching with dry straw or crop residues helps conserve soil moisture. Avoid over-irrigation, as it may cause flower drop. For better pod setting and uniform flowering, apply a foliar spray of 2% DAP solution or NAA @ 40 ppm during early flowering.

advisory_content: During the pod formation stage (43–56 DAS), maintaining adequate soil moisture is vital for proper pod development and grain filling in Blackgram. If there is no rainfall for 7–10 days, provide light to moderate irrigation (4–5 cm depth) using the furrow or basin method, avoiding waterlogging. Mulching with crop residues or straw helps conserve moisture and regulate soil temperature. Irrigate in the early morning or late afternoon to reduce evaporation losses and ensure good drainage. Monitor for pests like pod borer, aphids, whitefly, and tobacco caterpillar, which are active during this stage. Timely application of neem-based or recommended insecticides helps protect pods while maintaining optimal soil moisture.

advisory_content: Harvest management and Storage practices in Blackgram. Pods should be harvested at 70-80 % maturity when most of the pods turn black. Threshing is done manually or using machineries like vertical flow pulse thresher cum winnower and sun dried for 3 - 4 days to attain a moisture content of 8-10%. Apply edible oil @200 ml/quintal of grain or neem oil @200-250 ml / 50 kg of grain and mix it well to store for longer period. Mix neem leaves or begonia leaves to prevent storage pest attack @ 50g/kg of seeds.

After harvesting Blackgram, dry the seeds until the optimum moisture content is attained. Clean the entire surface where the storage is to be done. Thereafter, mix 250 ml of any edible oil per quintal of seed to reduce the infestation of pest during the storage. Dried leaves of either Begonia or Neem @ 1 kg/Bag can also be kept inside the storage bag to avoid pest infestation. To decrease the pest infestation during storage, dip the gunny bags in boiling water for 15min then dry it properly under the sun before filling the seeds in the bags. Else prepare Malathion 5ml per litre of water solution and then dip the gunny bags in it for 15min and then dry it properly under the sun. Thereafter store the seeds in the bags.

Important varieties, seed treatment and fertilizer management of mustard crop for odisha condition. This include NRCYS-05-02, NRCBH 101, Rajendra Suphalam, NDRE 7 (110 days), Pusa Bold, Pusa Bahar, Varuna (115 days), Pusa Mahak, Pusa Agrani, Pusa Jiakisan (95 days), Kranti (125 days). The seed rate for mustard cultivation is 3-4 kg/acre.

After selecting the land, plough the land for 3-4 times to loosen the soil through primary tillage to improve air circulation, drainage, and allow roots to penetrate deeper. Due to this clean the grass and roots of the previous crop. When the soil is having moisture sow mustard seeds in the field.

Generally, mustard is sown using a seed drill or by broadcasting them. It is important to thin seedlings to about 3 inches apart once they are established, and to ensure the soil is moist for germination If sown in rows, weeding, fertilizer application and pesticide spraying are convenient and cost-effective. Give 30 cm between rows and 10 cm between plants.

The seed rate for mustard cultivation is 3-4 kg/acre. Go for seed treatment before Sowing.Treat the seeds with Carbendazim 50% WP @ 2-gram/ kg of seeds or Carboxin 37.5 % + Thiram 37.5% D.S @ 3-gram/ kg of seeds. Plough the field 3 to 4 times and apply 2 tonnes of well decomposed FYM or Compost. Sow the seeds in line. Keep the row to row spacing of 30 cm and plant to plant spacing of 10 cm.

Fertilizer management in mustard. Plough the field 3 to 4 times and apply 2 tonnes of well decomposed FYM or Compost. Apply 100 kg of Gypsum per acre during final land preparation. For unirrigated condition where shorter duration varieties are grown apply 18-kg DAP, 28-kg Urea and 14 -kg of Murate of Potash before sowing of seeds and mix it properly to the soil. In irrigated condition where longer duration varieties are grown apply 22-kg of DAP, 13 kg of Urea (50%) and 17 kg of Murate of Potash. In soils that are deficient in sulfur and boron, 8 kg. of sulfur and 4 kg. of boron should be applied per acre. Sow the seeds in line. Keep the row to row spacing of 30 cm and plant to plant spacing of 10 cm.

Pre-emergence weed management in Mustard crop. To manage weeds in line sown Mustard, apply Isoproturon 50% WP @ 600-gram/acre or Oxyfluorfen 23.5 % EC @ 80ml/acre or Pendimethalin 30 % EC @ 750 ml/acre at 1-3 DAS. There should be sufficient moisture in soil during herbicide application. Go for thinning of crop at 15 DAS to maintain plant to plant spacing at 10 cm.

advisory_code: OD-ALL-RB-M-GS-Pest and disease (Mustard sawfly,Dry Root Rot)
advisory_title: Pest and disease (Mustard sawfly,Dry Root Rot)

advisory_content: If pre-emergence herbicide is not applied during sowing than to manage weeds apply post emergence herbicide like Quizalofop-Ethyl 5 % EC (Only for narrow weeds) @ 400-ml/acre should be applied at 15-20 DAS by mixing it in 200 litre of water. Always use flat-fan or flood-jet nozzle and clean water for herbicide spraying.

Interculture and nutrient management in irrigated mustard crop. Go for thinning of seedlings, removal of weak and diseased seedlings at 15 -20 DAS to maintain plant to plant spacing at 10 cm. Go for hoeing and hand weeding at 21 DAS and apply 21 kg of Urea in irrigated condition at this stage.

advisory_content: During the flowering stage (around 40–55 DAS) of mustard, maintaining adequate soil moisture is vital for good flower retention and pod setting. If there is no rainfall for 10–12 days, provide a light irrigation of 5–6 cm using furrow or check basin methods, ensuring that waterlogging is avoided to prevent Sclerotinia Stem rot and Alternaria blight. Irrigation should be done in the early morning or late afternoon to minimize evaporation losses. Light hoeing after irrigation and mulching with crop residues help conserve moisture and improve aeration, leading to better seed filling and yield.

advisory_code: OD-ALL-RB-M-F-Pest management (Leaf webber, Diamondback moth, Cabbage butterfly, Mustard aphid )-Disease Management (Alternaria blight , White rust, Downy mildew, Sclerotinia Stem rot, Dry Root Rot)

advisory_content: During the pod or siliqua formation stage (around 60–75 DAS) of mustard, adequate soil moisture is essential for proper pod filling and seed development. If there is no rainfall for 10–12 days, provide a light irrigation of 5–6 cm using furrow or check basin methods, ensuring that no water stagnates in the field to avoid root or stem rot. This stage is critical, as moisture stress can cause shrivelled seeds and yield loss. Irrigate during the early morning or late afternoon to reduce evaporation losses. Mulching with dry crop residues helps retain moisture and maintain soil temperature. Proper drainage should be maintained to prevent waterlogging and disease incidence such as Alternaria blight or White rust.

advisory_content: Harvest the crop as soon as the pods turn yellowish-brown. The crop is liable to shattering; hence it should be harvested just before the pods open to avoid losses. The harvested crop should be stacked in threshing floor for 5-days before threshing. After thrashing dry the seeds in sunlight for few days to bring the moisture content of seeds up to 8 %.

After threshing, dry the mustard seeds in sunlight for 3–4 days until the moisture content reaches about 8%. Clean the seeds to remove dust and chaff. Store the dried seeds in airtight gunny bags, metal bins, or plastic-lined containers and keep them in a cool, dry, and well-ventilated place away from damp walls and floor. For long-term storage, mix neem leaves or treat with Malathion 5% dust @ 250 g per 100 kg seed to protect from storage pests. Regularly check the stored seeds for moisture or insect attack.

Threshing is done manually or using machineries like vertical flow pulse thresher cum winnower and sun dried for 3 - 4 days to attain a moisture content of 8-10%. Apply edible oil @200 ml/quintal of grain or neem oil @200-250 ml / 50 kg of grain and mix it well to store for longer period. Mix neem leaves or begonia leaves to prevent storage pest attack @ 50g/kg of seeds.

After harvesting Greengram, dry the seeds until the optimum moisture content is attained. Clean the entire surface where the storage is to be done. Thereafter, mix 250 ml of any edible oil per quintal of seed to reduce the infestation of pest during the storage. Dried leaves of either Begonia or Neem @ 1 kg/Bag can also be kept inside the storage bag to avoid pest infestation. To decrease the pest infestation during storage, dip the gunny bags in boiling water for 15min then dry it properly under the sun before filling the seeds in the bags. Else prepare Malathion 5ml per litre of water solution and then dip the gunny bags in it for 15min and then dry it properly under the sun. Thereafter store the seeds in the bags.

Arrange good quality paddy seed varieties like Satyabhama, Sahabhagi Dhan, Mandakini, Manaswini, DRR 42, DRR-43, DRR-44, Bina 11, GB-1, Improved Lalat, MTU 1010, MTU 1001, Naveen, Swarna Shreya and Luna SanRBi (Coastal Saline) which can be grown during dry season. Seed rate for an acre of land is 20 kg for these varieties. Treat the seeds with Carbendazim 50% W.P @ 2-gram/1-kg of seeds or Carboxin 37.5% + Thiram 37.5 % @ 2- gram /1 kg of seeds (Dry treatment) before sowing in the nursery.

Irrigation should be done in nursery field and puddle it 2-3 times followed by planking. For one-acre area of paddy transplanting 400 m2 (10 Decimal) size of land is required for nursery raising. Apply 200 kg of FYM, 4 kg of DAP, 2.5 kg of MOP and 1 kg of Zinc sulphate during last puddling. Divide nursery area into smaller plots of 1.5-metre-long, 10 cm height and convenient length. Irrigation channels of size 30 cm in width should be made along the beds for irrigation/drainage purpose. Sprouted Seeds should be sown @ 40-50 gram/ m 2 area of seed bed by line sowing with 5 cm gap between each line or direct broadcasting.

advisory_code: OD-ALL-RB-TR-PDY-SGN-protection from cold injury to Nursery
advisory_content: During cool winter days to protect the rice nursery from cold injury, apply a thin layer of well decomposed FYM after sowing of seeds in wet bed to keep the bed relatively warm. Preferably use bore well water for irrigation at evening and drain out the cold water at morning to maintain the soil temperature favourable for growth. In severe cold affected areas use polythene cover during night.

advisory_code: OD-ALL-RB-TR-PDY-SGN- Pest and Irrigation management in Nursery
advisory_title: Nursery Management (+ Pest Management + Irrigation Management)
advisory_content: Apply 800-gram Cartap Hydrochloride 4% G  or 400-gram Chlorantraniliprole 0.4% G for 10 decimal nursery area 7 days before transplanting to manage gall midge, stem borer, caseworm, leaf folder and root knot nematode up to 3 weeks after transplanting. Standing water to a depth of 2-3 cm should be maintained at least for 2-3 days before uprooting of seedlings.

Farmers are advised for proper growth and development of paddy seedlings, balanced fertilizer application is essential, even in small nursery areas. In a 1 decimil (0.01 hectare) nursery plot, farmers are advised to apply about 40 kg of well-decomposed farmyard manure (FYM) or compost during land preparation to improve soil fertility and structure. Along with organic manure, chemical fertilizers should also be applied. As a basal dose, apply 1.0 kg of urea, 1.35 kg of Single Super Phosphate (SSP), and 375 grams of Muriate of Potash (MOP). These fertilizers should be mixed well into the soil 2–3 days before sowing, ensuring they are not applied in standing water. To further support seedling growth, apply 200 grams of urea as a top dressing around 15–20 days after sowing. Light irrigation should be done immediately after top dressing. This balanced approach helps promote strong root growth, healthy tillering, and vigorous seedlings ready for transplanting.

Farmers those who are going for transplanting should apply 35- kg DAP, 30-kg MOP and 8-kg Urea per acre during last puddling. For sandy soil apply 35 kg DAP, 15 kg MOP and 8 kg Urea per acre during last puddling. As per soil testing report, in Zinc deficient soil apply Zinc Sulphate (21%) @ 10 kg/acre at every three years. Transplanting of 25-30 days old seedlings should be done at a spacing of 20 x15 cm, plant 2-3 seedlings per hill for high yielding varieties.

advisory_title: Disease (BLB) /Pest (Yellow Stem borer, Thrips, Rice Hispa)

advisory_content: Use pre-emergence herbicide Pretilachlor 50% EW @ 500 ml/acre or Oxadiargyl 80%W. P @ 40-gm/acre or Pyrazosulfuron Ethyl 10% W.P @ 80-gm/acre or Redimix 6.15% (Pretilachlor 6% + Pyrazosulfuron Ethyl 0.15%) @ 4 kg/acre at 1-3 DAS. Spray the herbicide by mixing it in 200 litres of water per acre. Always use flat-fan or flood-jet nozzle and clean water for herbicide spraying.

Provide frequent and adequate irrigation to maintain sufficient soil moisture for puddling and transplanting. Apply 6–8 cm depth of water during field puddling to ensure good tilth and weed suppression. Maintain 2–3 cm standing water during and for 10 days after transplanting to aid seedling establishment. Avoid water stress by irrigating as soon as hairline cracks appear in the field. Where possible, use alternate furrow or plot irrigation to save water.

If rainfall is below 5 mm and RH drops below 70%, apply light irrigation (4–5 cm depth) 1–2 days before transplanting to ensure proper puddling and easy transplanting. Maintain standing water (2–3 cm) during and after transplanting for 7–10 days to help seedlings establish, then switch to alternate wetting and drying (AWD) to save water and prevent root rot.

Maintain approx. 2 cm standing water immediately after transplanting and for up to 7 days; afterward, apply Alternate Wetting and Drying (AWD) cycles—allow soil to dry until water table is ~10–15 cm below surface, then re-flood to 3–5 cm depth. Use water tube lysimeters where possible for monitoring.

In transplanted paddy to control grasses, sedges and broad leaf weeds farmers are advised to spray, apply Pretilachlor 6% GR + Bensulfuron-methyl 0.6% @ 4 kg/acre or Pyrazosulfuron ethyl 10% WP @ 80 gram or Triafamone 20%+ Ethoxy sulfuron 10% WG @ 90 gram or Almix @ 8 gram per acre at 10 -12 DAS in moist soil by mixing in 200-litre of water. Drain out excess water from paddy field before herbicide application and irrigate the field after two days of application of herbicide. Spray post-emergence herbicide like Bispyribac Sodium @ 120-ml/ acre or tank mix of Fenoxaprop-p-ethyl @ 400 ml + Ethoxysulfuron @ 200 ml/acre at 15-20 DAS in moist soil (When the weeds are at 3-4 leaf stage) by mixing in 200-litre of water. Always use clean water for herbicide application. Use flat fan nozzle or flood jet nozzle for herbicide application.

Maintain 3–5 cm of standing water in the field for optimal tiller development. Avoid long dry intervals; irrigate as soon as surface cracks appear. Ensure uniform field leveling to prevent uneven water distribution. Adopt Alternate Wetting and Drying (AWD) only after sufficient tiller formation. Light irrigation should be given after each intercultural or fertilizer operation to maintain soil moisture balance.

Maintain 2–3 cm of standing water depending on soil moisture condition. Irrigate after 3–4 days of no rainfall or if upper soil layer starts drying. Prefer Alternate Wetting and Drying (AWD) method — re-irrigate when hairline cracks appear. Avoid over-irrigation to prevent nutrient leaching and lodging. Schedule light irrigation after top dressing of fertilizers to aid nutrient absorption.

Maintain a standing water depth of 2–5 cm. Begin with continuous submergence, then transition to AWD: when water falls ~15 cm below soil surface (monitored via a pipe), re-flood to ~5 cm. Prioritize this from tillering to panicle initiation.

advisory_content: Farmers are advised to apply fertilizer in Paddy crop during active tillering stage. For high yielding paddy varieties, apply 35 kg Urea per acre and for hybrids apply 42-kg of Urea per acre 21 days after transplanting at active tillering stage. Before applying urea in the field, you should complete weed control operations and drain out access water from the field.

advisory_title: Diseases (BLB, Brown Spot, Sheath blight, Foot rot),Pests (Yellow Stem borer, Leaf Folder, Caseworm, Rice hispa, BPH, Swarming caterpillar)

advisory_content: Fertilizer management at PI stage. At PI stage of paddy crop, apply 20 kg of Urea per acre. For Light sandy soil apply 20 kg Urea and 15 Kg of Murate of Potash per acre. Again, remember at PI stage of paddy crop apply 20 kg of Urea per acre. For Light sandy soil apply 20 kg Urea and 15 Kg of Murate of Potash per acre.

Maintain continuous standing water (~5 cm) during panicle initiation to reproductive stages. If using AWD, re-irrigate when water drops ~15 cm below soil surface, reflood to ~5 cm. These water levels significantly improve leaf area, growth rate, and yield.

if rainfall is between 10–25 mm with moderate humidity (60–75%) and warm temperatures (30–33°C), provide light irrigation of 4–5 cm depth to maintain consistent soil moisture. Keep the field moist but not flooded. Avoid water stress, as moisture deficit during this stage can reduce panicle initiation and grain number. Schedule irrigation at 5–7 day intervals, depending on soil type and rainfall forecast.

If rainfall (25–50 mm) and high humidity (70–85%) prevail with 3–5 rainy days, additional irrigation is not immediately required. Maintain a shallow water layer of 2–3 cm and avoid over-irrigation to prevent nutrient leaching and lodging. Ensure proper drainage in low-lying areas to avoid water stagnation.

advisory_content: Maintain a shallow standing water level (~2–5 cm) continuously. Avoid excessive inundation (>5 cm) during booting and maturity, as it can accelerate root decay, leaf senescence, delay heading, reduce filled grains, and lower harvest index Map My Crop. AWD is an acceptable alternative but must ensure water does not drop below “safe” thresholds—i.e., re-flood when water falls ~15 cm below surface to maintain ~5 cm depth Wikipedia. Moderation in irrigation, combined with nitrogen management, enhances productivity and grain quality

If rainfall is insufficient, apply light to moderate irrigation (4–5 cm depth) to maintain adequate soil moisture at the root zone. Avoid waterlogging to prevent fungal diseases. Irrigation is best applied in the early morning or late afternoon to reduce evaporation losses.

Light irrigation may be applied only if soil moisture in the root zone is insufficient. The aim is to maintain adequate moisture without overwatering. Irrigate preferably in the early morning or late afternoon. Avoid waterlogging, which can increase disease incidence like sheath blight.

advisory_content: Maintain continuous shallow flooding (~5 cm) during flowering to protect pollen viability and stigma receptivity; avoid water stress and temperature spikes. Use AWD cautiously: only allow water level to drop below soil surface after flowering, not during.

Apply moderate irrigation to maintain optimum soil moisture for proper flowering and pollination. Avoid waterlogging and ensure uniform moisture in the root zone. Schedule irrigation for early morning or late afternoon to minimize evaporation and reduce disease risk such as blast or bacterial leaf blight.

Apply light irrigation only if the soil moisture in the root zone is insufficient. Maintain uniform moisture to avoid moisture stress, which can reduce spikelet fertility and grain filling. Prefer irrigation in early morning or late afternoon and avoid standing water to prevent disease incidence such as blast or bacterial leaf blight.

advisory_content: Begin field drainage approximately 10–15 days before harvest to ensure uniform ripening, facilitate harvesting, and improve milling quality; maintain adequate soil moisture to avoid stress during ripening. Do not apply further irrigation at full maturity.

Apply moderate irrigation to maintain adequate moisture for grain filling and proper maturity. Avoid excess water, as over-irrigation may delay maturity or increase disease incidence. Irrigate early in the morning or late afternoon to reduce evaporation and maintain field drainage. Ensure uniform soil moisture across the field for consistent grain development.

Only light irrigation is required or can be skipped if adequate rainfall has occurred recently. Ensure proper drainage to avoid waterlogging, as excess moisture at this stage may affect grain quality and promote diseases. Focus on maintaining uniform soil moisture to allow proper grain filling and timely crop maturity.

Harvest management in paddy. Drain out the water from paddy field, 15 days before harvesting of the crop. Harvest the rice crop at physiological maturity (85% grains matured in a panicle) and looks pale yellow in colour. Harvest the crop by leaving one metre from the bund if grain is to be kept for seed purpose. Dry the harvested paddy grains in direct sunlight for 2-3 days to get the moisture content at 14%. For seed purpose the grain should be dried to below 12% moisture content.

After harvest, dry the grains in sunlight to reduce the moisture content of the grains. Different insects like grain weevil, lesser grain borer, etc attack the stored grains. During the rainy season, if the grains become slightly damp, the weevils make holes and feed inside the rice grains. The larva of the lesser grain borer makes hole in the grain and feeds inside the grains. Clean the storage area and provide proper ventilation. Fumigate the grains with Aluminium phosphide 56% @ 3 tablets per10 Qt of grains. Disinfect the storage bags with Malathion 50 EC @ 10 ml/l.

Rat control in paddy field. At ripening stage in paddy field, rats cause serious damage like cutting rice panicle, eating ripe grains etc. At primary stage, apply dry chilli smoke in rat hole. Farmers can use dry fish trap, sickle trap or arrow trap for rat control. For preparation of 100 g poison bait, mix wheat flour, mustard oil and jagerry powder and add Zinc phosphide @ 3 g. Apply it to rat way or near the bond or in rat hole. Instead of this farmer can also apply Celphos pouch wrapping in wet cotton @ 1 per hole. Then plaster the hole with mud.

Soil testing is essential for understanding soil fertility and planning balanced fertilizer application. Farmers are advised to collect soil samples 15-20 days before sowing any crop. For accurate results, divide the field into different uniform areas based on crop history, soil type, or slope. From each area, collect 8-10 sub-samples in a zig-zag pattern using a spade or auger. Remove the top 2 cm of soil and collect samples from a depth of 15-20 cm. Mix the sub-samples thoroughly in a clean bucket and take about 500 grams of the composite sample. Dry it in shade (not in direct sun), crush large lumps, and remove stones or debris. Label the sample with farmerâ€™s name, field location, and previous crop. Send the sample to the nearest soil testing laboratory. A proper soil test helps in applying the right amount of fertilizers and saves cost while increasing crop yield.

Summer ploughing is an important practice before the kharif season to improve soil health and prepare the land for sowing. Farmers are advised to start ploughing during hot summer months (April-June) when the soil is dry, and temperatures range between 30-40Â°C. Plough the field 2-3 times using a mouldboard or disc plough to break hard soil layers and expose pests, weed seeds, and disease-causing organisms to the heat, which helps in natural pest control. Ensure the soil has no standing water during ploughing. Allow 7-10 days between each ploughing for proper drying. Summer ploughing also improves soil aeration and enhances moisture retention for the upcoming monsoon. It is especially beneficial in areas that receive early monsoon showers, as it allows quicker sowing of kharif crops. Farmers should complete summer ploughing before the onset of regular rains to make the soil ready for green manuring or nursery establishment.

Green manure crops like Dhaincha (Sesbania aculeata) and Sesbania should be sown during the pre-kharif season to improve soil fertility before transplanting the main crop, such as paddy. Farmers are advised to prepare the land by ploughing 2-3 times to achieve a fine tilth. Ensure adequate soil moisture through pre-monsoon showers or provide one light irrigation before sowing. Use 20-25 kg/ha of seed for Dhaincha or 25-30 kg/ha for Sesbania, sown at a depth of 2-3 cm. Seeds may be broadcasted or line-sown with 25-30 cm spacing. Maintain soil moisture through light irrigation if rainfall is inadequate. Grow the crop for 6-8 weeks and incorporate it into the soil before flowering, when plants reach 40-60 cm height. Use a plough or rotavator to mix the green biomass into the soil, and allow 7-10 days for decomposition before transplanting the paddy crop.

Acid soils (pH below 5.5) reduce crop productivity by affecting nutrient availability and increasing toxicity of elements like aluminum and iron. Farmers are advised to get their soil tested to know the exact pH. To manage acidic soils, apply lime (CaCOâ‚ƒ) or dolomite based on soil test recommendations. As a general guide, 2-4 quintals (200-400 kg) of lime per acre is applied once every 3-4 years. Lime should be mixed thoroughly in the soil 15-20 days before sowing, preferably during land preparation. Apply lime in moist soil, not in standing water, and never mix it directly with fertilizers or organic manure. Along with liming, apply organic matter like compost or green manure to improve soil health. Regular liming and good crop rotation help restore soil productivity and maintain long-term soil health.

For up land ecosystem varieties which matures early. For Upland ecosystem, varieties which mature between 90-120 days are suitable for cultivation varieties like Ankit, Khandagiri, Sahabhagi Dhan, Mandakini, Naveen, CO-51, Swarna sakhti dhan, Swarna Shreya, OUAT Kalinga Rice-5 etc can be cultivated in bunded upland condition.

For medium land, varieties which mature in between 120 to 140 days are suitable. Varieties like MTU 1156 (Tarangini), MTU 1153 (Chandra), MTU 1064, MTU- 1190, Swarna samridhi dhan, CR dhan- 307 (maudamani), CR Dhan 310, CR Dhan 312, CR Dhan 315, , Lalat, Improved Lalat, MTU-1001, MTU-1010, Bina-11, OUAT Kalinga Rice-1,2,3,4,7,8 etc can be cultivated in medium land.

For Low land ecosystem varieties which matures above 140 days are suitable. Varieties like Varsha dhan, Pratikshya, Swarna Sub-1, MTU-1210, MTU-1224, MTU-1140, MTU-1239, MTU-1262, MTU-1061 (Indra), RGL-2537, MTU-1075, Ashutosh, Hasanta, Pratibha, Mrinalini, MTU 7029, CR 1018, CR 1009 Sub-1, Pooja, CR dhan 409 (Pradhan Dhan), Sarla, Kalachampa, OUAT Kalinga Rice-6 etc can be cultivated in low land.

advisory_title: Fair Average quality seed(Good quality seed priming)
advisory_content: Good quality seed reduces the necessary seed rate, provides healthy and strong seedlings, gives a uniform crop stand in the field, and results in higher yields. Good quality seed is clean (contains no stones, soil particles, weed seed), pure (contains only grains from one variety), and healthy (full big grains, same colour, no cracks). Therefore, get the certified paddy seeds from your nearest PACS or LAMPS or registered Dealer. If you are using the own harvested seeds, then dissolve 1 kg salt in 10 litres of water for 10 kg of seed. Dip 2-3 kg of the seed at a time in the solution and reject the floating chaffy and weed seeds. Collect heavy seeds settled at bottom of the solution and wash them 2-3 times by clean water. Dry the seeds in direct sunlight for 2-3 days before treating them with fungicides.

Nursery bed preparation for dry nursery and sowing may be done after monsoon showers in rainfed transplanted rice areas. For one-acre area of paddy transplanting 400 m2 (10 Decimal) size of land is required for nursery raising. Selected nursery field should be ploughed 3- 4 times. Apply 1 tonne of FYM and mixed it properly during last ploughing. Divide nursery area into smaller plots of 1.5-metre-long, 10 cm height and convenient length. Irrigation channels of size 30 cm in breadth should be made along the beds for irrigation/drainage. For each square metre of nursery bed apply 10 gram of DAP and 10 grams of MOP. For each square metre of nursery bed 50 to 60 grams of treated seeds should be sown in line at 5 cm gap and at a depth of 3 cm.

Farmers are advised to go for wet bed nursery raising for paddy cultivation. For one-acre area of paddy transplanting 400 m2 (10 Decimal) size of land is required for nursery raising. Irrigation should be done in nursery field and puddle it 2-3 times followed by planking. Apply 200 kg of FYM, 4 kg of DAP, 2.5 kg of MOP and 1 kg of Zinc sulphate during last puddling. Divide nursery area into smaller plots of 1.5-metre-long, 10 cm height and convenient length. Irrigation channels of size 30 cm in width should be made along the beds for irrigation/drainage. Sprouted Seeds should be sown @ 40-50 gram/ m2 area of seed bed by line sowing with 5 cm gap between each line or direct broadcasting and put dried compost over seeds Apply light irrigations to the nursery area particularly in the evening so that the field remains wet and do not keep standing water.

Farmers are advised for proper growth and development of paddy seedlings, balanced fertilizer application is essential, even in small nursery areas. In a 1 decimil (0.01 hectare) nursery plot, farmers are advised to apply about 40 kg of well-decomposed farmyard manure (FYM) or compost during land preparation to improve soil fertility and structure. Along with organic manure, chemical fertilizers should also be applied. As a basal dose, apply 1.0 kg of urea, 1.35 kg of Single Super Phosphate (SSP), and 375 grams of Muriate of Potash (MOP). These fertilizers should be mixed well into the soil 2-3 days before sowing, ensuring they are not applied in standing water. To further support seedling growth, apply 200 grams of urea as a top dressing around 15-20 days after sowing. Light irrigation should be done immediately after top dressing. This balanced approach helps promote strong root growth, healthy tillering, and vigorous seedlings ready for transplanting.

Rainfall is expected to be less than required in the coming week, which will not be sufficient to meet the water needs of paddy nurseries. Therefore, farmers are advised to provide irrigation every 2 to 3 days to maintain a standing water level of 2 to 5 cm in the nursery beds. This will support proper germination and healthy seedling development. It is best to irrigate during early morning or late evening hours to minimize water loss due to evaporation. Avoid excess watering to prevent seedling damage. Ensure proper bunding and drainage to conserve water and avoid nutrient loss.

Rainfall is expected to be normal in the coming week, which will be sufficient to meet the water needs of paddy nurseries. Therefore, farmers are advised to watch the field regularly to maintain a standing water level of 2 to 5 cm in the nursery beds. Avoid excess watering to prevent seedling damage. Ensure proper bunding and drainage to conserve water and avoid nutrient loss.

Rainfall is expected to be higher than normal in the coming week, which may lead to excess water in paddy nurseries. Farmers are advised to closely monitor water levels and avoid water stagnation beyond 5 cm, as it can damage young seedlings and affect germination. Ensure proper drainage channels are in place to remove excess water from the nursery beds. Strengthen bunds to prevent waterlogging and soil erosion. In low-lying areas, create outlets to safely drain water. Avoid any irrigation until water levels return to normal. Timely removal of excess water will help protect seedlings and promote healthy nursery growth.

Farmers who are going for transplanting should apply 35 kg DAP, 27 kg MOP and 4-kg Urea per acre during last puddling for high yielding paddy varieties as basal fertilizer. For sandy soil apply 35 kg DAP, 15 kg Potash and 4 kg Urea per acre during last puddling. If soil is Zinc deficient as per soil testing report then apply Zinc Sulphate (21%) @ 10 kg/acre for sandy soil and 5 kg/acre for clay soil at every three years. Transplanting of 20-25 days old seedlings should be done at a spacing of 20x15 cm, plant 2-3 seedlings per hill for high yielding varieties.

farmers are advised to control the weeds go for pre-emergence herbicide in paddy crop. Use pre-emergence herbicide Pretilachlor 37 % EW @ 600-ml/acre or Bensulfuron methyl 0.6% GR + Pretilachlor 6% GR @ 4 kg/acre or Pretilachlor 6% GR + Pyrazosulfuron Ethyl 0.15% GR @ 4 kg/acre or Pyrazosulfuron Ethyl 10% W.P @ 80-gm/acre at 1-3 DAT. Spray the herbicide by mixing it in 200 litres of water per acre or mix the herbicide with 20 kg of sand and broadcast it uniformly. Always use flat-fan or flood-jet nozzle and clean water for herbicide spraying.

The forecast indicates low rainfall below 30 mm, with maximum temperatures (Tmax) of 38Â°C, minimum temperatures (Tmin) around 25Â°C, and relative humidity (RH) between 50-60%. Such conditions increase evaporation and soil moisture loss. Farmers with transplanted paddy are advised to provide irrigation every 2-3 days to maintain a standing water level of 3-5 cm in the field, especially during the critical early establishment phase. Avoid water stress at this stage to prevent transplant shock and promote tillering. Use field bunding to retain water and minimize losses.

With moderate rainfall between 30-70 mm, Tmax of 32-35Â°C, Tmin of 23-25Â°C, and RH ranging from 65-80%, conditions are favorable for transplanted paddy. Natural rainfall will generally meet water requirements, but farmers should monitor field conditions. Maintain a 3-5 cm water level and avoid over-flooding. Drain excess water after moderate showers to improve root aeration and reduce the risk of fungal diseases. Nutrient application and weeding operations can be scheduled during dry intervals.

Forecasted heavy rainfall over 70 mm, along with Tmax of 29-32Â°C, Tmin of 22-24Â°C, and high RH levels of 85-95%, may lead to waterlogging in transplanted paddy fields. In such conditions, stop all irrigation and focus on drainage management. Ensure all field drains and bund outlets are open to remove excess water and prevent root suffocation or disease outbreak. Monitor for blast and sheath blight due to high humidity. Do not apply fertilizers during heavy rain; wait for the field to slightly dry before top dressing. Maintaining a proper drainage system is crucial to avoid yield loss during prolonged rainfall.

Farmers who have transplanted or broadcasted, should spray post-emergence herbicide like Bispyribac Sodium @ 80-ml/ acre or tank mix of Fenoxaprop-p-ethyl 9.3 % W/W EC @ 250 ml + Ethoxysulfuron 15 % WDG @ 50 g /acre at 15-20 DAS in moist soil (When the weeds are at 3-4 leaf stage) by mixing in 200-litre of water. Drain out water from paddy field before spraying of herbicide and irrigate the field after 2-3 days. Apply sticker @ 5 ml/15 lit with herbicide. Always use clean water for herbicide application. Use flat fan nozzle or flood jet nozzle for herbicide application.

advisory_title: Main field plant disease & pest (BLB ,Brown Spot, Stem borer, Rice hispa, Blast)

advisory_content: Rainfall is expected to be lower than normal in the coming week, which may not meet the water requirements of paddy during the vegetative stage. Farmers are advised to irrigate the field every 4-5 days to maintain a standing water level of 5-7 cm. This is a critical stage for tiller formation and plant growth, so do not allow water stress. Irrigation should be done preferably in the early morning or late evening to reduce water loss through evaporation. Maintain bunds and repair any leaks to conserve water. Avoid deep flooding, as it can restrict root growth. Timely weeding and nutrient management should continue as per schedule.

Rainfall is expected to be normal during the week, which should be sufficient for the vegetative growth of paddy. Farmers are advised to monitor water levels and ensure a consistent 5-7 cm of standing water in the field. Avoid unnecessary irrigation. Ensure bunds are intact to retain water, and clean field channels to allow quick drainage after moderate rains. Weed control and top-dressing of urea should be done during dry periods. Balanced nutrient management and pest monitoring should continue during this favorable weather window.

Rainfall is expected to be higher than normal in the coming week, which may lead to waterlogging in paddy fields. Farmers should avoid any further irrigation and ensure excess water is drained out promptly to maintain 5-7 cm standing water and prevent submergence. Prolonged water stagnation can damage roots and reduce tiller production. Clean drainage channels and strengthen bunds to prevent soil erosion and nutrient loss. In low-lying or flood-prone areas, create temporary outlets or ridges for safe drainage. Monitor the crop for diseases like leaf blast or bacterial leaf blight, which thrive in high humidity and wet conditions.

Farmers are advised to apply fertilizer in Paddy crop during active tillering stage. For high yielding paddy varieties, apply 35 kg Urea per acre and for hybrids apply 42-kg of Urea per acre 21 days after transplanting at active tillering stage. Before applying urea in the field, you should complete weed control operations and drain out access water from the field.

advisory_title: Pests (Stem borer, Leaf Folder ,caseworm, Rice hispa, Swarming caterpillar)

advisory_content:
In the upcoming week, rainfall is expected to be below normal and insufficient to meet the water requirements of the paddy crop at the panicle initiation stage. This is a critical growth phase where the formation of panicles begins, and any moisture stress can lead to poor grain development and yield loss. The maximum temperature is expected to range between 32-34Â°C, minimum temperature around 23-25Â°C, with relative humidity between 65-75%.
Farmers are advised to irrigate the crop every 4-5 days and maintain a standing water level of about 5 cm in the field. Irrigation should be scheduled during the early morning or late evening to minimize water loss through evaporation. Proper bunding must be ensured to retain water effectively and avoid seepage. This will help maintain soil moisture and promote healthy panicle development.

Rainfall is expected to be normal in the coming week, which should adequately support the water requirement of paddy during the panicle initiation stage. Maximum temperatures may range from 30-32Â°C, minimum from 22-24Â°C, and relative humidity is likely to stay between 75-85%.
Under these conditions, frequent irrigation may not be necessary. However, farmers are advised to monitor the field daily and ensure a consistent water level of 5 cm. Natural rainfall should be efficiently utilized by maintaining strong bunds and preventing overflow or water loss. Fertilizer applications, if due, should be timed during dry spells. Regular field observation is essential to adjust irrigation based on rainfall and soil moisture.

Heavy rainfall is expected during the week, with maximum temperature ranging between 28-30Â°C, minimum between 21-23Â°C, and high relative humidity levels around 85-95%. Such conditions may lead to waterlogging, which can negatively affect the crop at the sensitive panicle initiation stage.
Farmers should stop irrigation immediately and focus on draining excess water from the fields. Continuous water stagnation beyond 5 cm can restrict nutrient uptake and increase the risk of fungal diseases. Drainage channels must be cleaned and maintained to allow free flow of water. Strengthen field bunds and open temporary outlets in case of heavy accumulation of rainwater. Timely removal of excess water will help protect the crop and support healthy panicle formation.

Fertilizer management at PI stage. At PI stage of paddy crop, apply 20 kg of Urea per acre. For Light sandy soil apply 20 kg Urea and 15 Kg of Murate of Potash per acre. Again, remember at PI stage of paddy crop apply 20 kg of Urea per acre. For Light sandy soil apply 20 kg Urea and 15 Kg of Murate of Potash per acre.

advisory_title: GLH Stem borer, Leaf Folder ,Caseworm, Gundhi bug

advisory_content: In the coming week, rainfall is expected to be below normal, with maximum temperature between 32-34Â°C, minimum temperature around 23-25Â°C, and relative humidity between 60-70%. At the booting or pre-flowering stage, paddy plants are preparing for flowering, and any water stress can lead to flower sterility and poor grain filling. Farmers are advised to provide irrigation every 3-4 days to maintain a standing water level of 5-7 cm in the field. Ensure bunds are well-maintained to retain water efficiently. Irrigation should be done in the early morning or late evening to reduce evaporation losses. Avoid alternate wetting and drying at this stage, as the crop requires consistent moisture for panicle exertion and pollination.

With normal rainfall expected, maximum temperatures are likely to range from 30-32Â°C, minimum around 22-24Â°C, and relative humidity between 75-85%. This is generally favorable for paddy during the booting stage. Farmers are advised to monitor the field regularly to ensure a water level of 5 cm is maintained. If rainfall is well distributed, irrigation may not be needed, but excessive or prolonged water stagnation should be avoided. Make sure bunds are intact and field drains are functional to prevent temporary flooding. Schedule any remaining fertilizer topdressing before panicle emergence and ensure field operations do not disturb the crop at this delicate stage.

Heavy rainfall is predicted, with maximum temperatures between 28-30Â°C, minimum around 21-23Â°C, and relative humidity above 90%. Such wet and humid conditions can cause waterlogging, which may damage the roots and reduce oxygen availability during a critical reproductive phase. Farmers are advised to stop irrigation immediately and focus on draining excess water from the field. Maintain water levels at no more than 5 cm, and avoid complete submergence of the crop. Ensure all field channels and drainage outlets are clear. In low-lying fields, take extra precautions to prevent water stagnation. Monitor for diseases like sheath blight and neck blast, which are common under such high humidity.

advisory_title: Pests (Stem borer, Earhead Caterpillar, Leaf Folder, GLH, Gundhi bug, BPH)

advisory_content: In the upcoming week, rainfall is expected to be below 30 mm, which is insufficient to meet the water requirements of paddy during its critical flowering and pollination stage. The expected maximum temperature ranges between 32-34Â°C, minimum between 23-25Â°C, and relative humidity is likely to be 60-70%. This stage is highly sensitive to moisture stress, and even a short period of dryness can lead to spikelet sterility, affecting yield. Farmers are advised to irrigate every 3-4 days, maintaining a standing water level of 5-7 cm in the field. Irrigation should be done early in the morning or late evening to reduce evaporation. Proper bunding is essential to avoid seepage, and field operations should be minimized to prevent plant disturbance during flowering.

Rainfall in the coming week is expected to be within the normal range (30â€“80 mm), which should sufficiently support the paddy crop at its flowering stage. The maximum temperature is likely to range from 30â€“32Â°C, the minimum around 22â€“24Â°C, with relative humidity between 75â€“85%. These are favorable conditions for flowering and pollination. In this case, farmers should regularly monitor field moisture and maintain a 5 cm standing water level. If rainfall is well-distributed, additional irrigation may not be necessary. However, avoid overwatering and water stagnation, which could lead to disease. Ensure that bunds are intact and drainage is functional. Avoid any field activities that may interfere with the delicate pollination process.

Heavy rainfall is expected in the week ahead, with total precipitation exceeding 80 mm. The forecast shows maximum temperatures between 28â€“30Â°C, minimum temperatures around 21â€“23Â°C, and relative humidity exceeding 90%. These wet and humid conditions can cause waterlogging, which negatively affects flowering and grain setting. Farmers are advised to stop all irrigation activities and focus on draining excess water from the field to maintain water levels below 5â€“6 cm. Open drainage channels and clear blockages to prevent water accumulation. Continuous stagnation can lead to spikelet sterility and increase the risk of fungal diseases like neck blast. In low-lying areas, special care should be taken to protect the crop, and preventive plant protection measures may be required under expert advice.

advisory_title: Pests (Stem borer, Leaf Folder, , Gundhi bug, WBPH)

advisory_content:
Rainfall in the upcoming week is expected to be less than 30 mm, with maximum temperatures between 32-34Â°C, minimum temperatures around 23-25Â°C, and relative humidity of 60-70%, then the crop is at risk of moisture stress. Dry conditions at this stage can lead to poor grain filling and chaffy grains.
Farmers are advised to irrigate every 5-6 days to maintain a shallow water level of 3-5 cm in the field. It is important not to allow the soil to dry completely, as consistent soil moisture supports nutrient movement and healthy grain development. Ensure bunds are strong to hold irrigation water effectively. Irrigation should be done either early in the morning or in the evening to reduce evaporation losses.

It is forecasted that their is a normal rainfall (30-80 mm/week), with maximum temperatures of 30-32Â°C, minimum temperatures around 22-24Â°C, and relative humidity between 75-85%, the weather is favorable for proper grain filling.
Farmers should closely monitor soil moisture, especially during any brief dry spell. A light standing water layer of about 2-3 cm is sufficient at this stage. If the rainfall is evenly distributed, irrigation might not be necessary. However, timely drainage should be ensured if there is excessive accumulation of water. Proper bunding and weed-free fields will help conserve moisture and avoid unnecessary losses.

It is  heavy rainfall (>80 mm/week) is forecasted, with maximum temperatures around 28-30Â°C, minimum temperatures 21-23Â°C, and relative humidity above 90%, there is a serious risk of waterlogging. Continuous stagnation during grain filling can affect oxygen supply to roots, increase the risk of fungal diseases, and lower grain quality.
Farmers should immediately stop any irrigation and focus on draining excess rainwater from the field. Standing water should be kept below 3 cm. Ensure all drainage channels are open and functional, especially in low-lying areas. High humidity also increases the chances of diseases such as false smut or sheath rot, so regular field monitoring is recommended, and plant protection measures should be applied if symptoms appear.

advisory_title: Diseases (BLB, Sheath blight, Neck blast, , False smut)
Pests (Stem borer, Leaf Folder, , Gundhi bug, WBPH, Panicle mite)

advisory_content: The forecasted weekly rainfall is expected to be below 30 mm, and the temperature ranges between 33-35Â°C (max) and 22-24Â°C (min) with relative humidity around 60-70%, soil moisture levels may become inadequate to support final grain development. In such conditions, farmers are advised to apply one light irrigation of 2-3 cm during the early dough stage to prevent stress and ensure proper grain hardening. After this stage, no further irrigation should be given, allowing the field to dry naturally in preparation for harvest. This also helps in reducing grain moisture content and eases harvesting. Care should be taken not to over-irrigate, as excessive moisture can lead to lodging, fungal diseases, or delayed ripening.

With expected rainfall between 30 and 80 mm, temperatures of 30-32Â°C (max) and 21-23Â°C (min), and relative humidity between 75-85%, the environment typically supports a healthy grain hardening and ripening process. Farmers should closely observe soil moisture; if rainfall is well distributed, additional irrigation may not be necessary. However, if a dry spell occurs, one light irrigation may be applied during the dough stage to avoid moisture stress. As the crop reaches maturity, water should be allowed to recede naturally, and no further irrigation should be done. This helps promote uniform grain ripening and prepares the field for smooth harvesting without causing post-harvest issues like grain sprouting or discoloration.

In heavy rainfall is forecasted, exceeding 80 mm per week, with temperatures around 28-30Â°C (max) and 20-22Â°C (min), and relative humidity above 90%, then excess water in the field becomes a major concern. Prolonged water stagnation at the maturity stage can result in poor grain quality, sprouting of grains on the panicle, lodging, and difficulties in harvesting. In such cases, farmers must focus on effective drainage management. Immediate steps should be taken to remove excess water from the field through field channels and outlets. Strengthening bunds and cleaning drainage paths is essential to prevent waterlogging. Fields should be monitored closely, and harvesting should be planned when 80-85% of the grains turn golden yellow and grain moisture content is below 20% to ensure quality and minimize losses.

Harvest management in paddy. Drain out the water from paddy field, 15 days before harvesting of the crop. Harvest the rice crop at physiological maturity (85% grains matured in a panicle) and looks pale yellow in colour. Harvest the crop by leaving one metre from the bund if grain is to be kept for seed purpose. Dry the harvested paddy grains in direct sunlight for 2-3 days to get the moisture content at 14%. For seed purpose the grain should be dried to below 12% moisture content.

After harvest, dry the grains in sunlight to reduce the moisture content of the grains. Different insects like grain weevil, lesser grain borer, etc attack the stored grains. During the rainy season, if the grains become slightly damp, the weevils make holes and feed inside the rice grains. The larva of the lesser grain borer makes hole in the grain and feeds inside the grains. Clean the storage area and provide proper ventilation. Fumigate the grains with Aluminium phosphide 56% @ 3 tablets per10 Qt of grains. Disinfect the storage bags with Malathion 50 EC @ 10 ml/l.

Rat control in paddy field. At ripening stage in paddy field, rats cause serious damage like cutting rice panicle, eating ripe grains etc. At primary stage, apply dry chilli smoke in rat hole. Farmers can use dry fish trap, sickle trap or arrow trap for rat control. For preparation of 100 g poison bait, mix wheat flour, mustard oil and jagerry powder and add Zinc phosphide @ 3 g. Apply it to rat way or near the bond or in rat hole. Instead of this farmer can also apply Celphos pouch wrapping in wet cotton @ 1 per hole. Then plaster the hole with mud.

Some high yielding Potato varieties are Kufri lalima, Kufri Pukhraj, Baby potato, Kufri Jyoti, Kufri Badsah and Kufri Sundari, Kufri Khyaati, Kufri Suriya, Chipsona. For potato planting, disease free, well-sprouted and treated small whole potato tubers are used. The tuber seed should be 20-25 gram each in weight. Depending upon the size of seeds for one acre of potato cultivation 6-8 quintals of tuber seeds required.

Treat the seeds before planting. For treatment of tuber seed, first make a solution of Carbendazim 25% + Mancozeb 50% WS @ 300-gram and Imidacloprid @ 30 ml by mixing it in 100-litres of water.  Dip one quintal of the tuber seeds in this solution for 20-25 minutes. Remove the tuber seeds from the solution and keep it in shade to dry for 2-4 hours before planting.

One month before planting 4-5 ploughings are done to get fine tilth and 6-8 tons well decomposed compost along with 10 kg chlorpyriphos dust added before ridge making. For planting small potato seeds spacing is to be maintained at 45-60 c.m. x 15-22 c.m. For this mechanical potato planter is used. Basal fertilizers are applied by making 6-7 c.m. deep furrows in between ridges. Cover the applied fertilizer with 1-2 cm of FYM and then plant the potato seeds with further covering in soil.

In Rabi, natural rainfall is minimal (0–5 mm); pre-planting irrigation is essential to bring the soil to field capacity. Ensure fine tilth and uniform moisture (70–85% RH) for proper planting. Provide a light irrigation within 3–5 days of planting, especially if rainfall is absent, to support uniform sprouting but avoid water stagnation, especially in medium to heavy soils. In sandy/light soils, consider a second light irrigation after 5–7 days if no rainfall occurs. Use furrow irrigation for better moisture control and to prevent direct contact of water with seed tubers. Ensure good surface drainage to prevent waterlogging, which can cause seed tuber rot and poor emergence.

Apply 32kg Urea, 52 kg DAP and 40 kg MOP as basal dose per acre in the furrows, having 60 cm distance, mix the fertilizer with soil and then put the seed tubers on the furrow at spacing of 20 cm plant to plant.

Irrigation is crucial as ambient temperature is moderate and rainfall is low. Provide light irrigation immediately after planting and again after 5–7 days interval to ensure uniform sprouting and growth. Avoid waterlogging.

advisory_title: Pest and Disease management (Common scab, Cutworm, Black leg)

advisory_content: Go for hoeing and hand weeding at 21 DAS and earthing up should be done by applying 26 Kg of Urea and 20 Kg of MOP in line on both side at 3-4 cm from the plants and at a depth of 2-3 cm. After 3 weeks of first earthing up go for hoeing and hand weeding and apply again 26 Kg of Urea and 20 Kg of MOP in line on both side the plants and earthing up should be done to cover up the tubers properly. The ridges should be high and strong enough to cover up tubers. This will help increase in water holding capacity and proper growth of plants and tubers. Potato turns green when seen above ground and exposed to direct sun light, therefore, it should be covered with soil immediately. Only lower Two-third of the ridge should be submerged during irrigation. If the potato field is waterlogged, the yield is reduced. Pre-emergence application of metribuzin @150g/acre keeps the potato crop weed free for about 25 days.

advisory_title: Pest and Disease management (Early blight, Bacterial wilt, Aphids, Whitefly, Thrips, Leafhopper, Cutworm)

advisory_content: This is the most critical stage for yield. Begin irrigation at shorter intervals (7–10 days), especially if rainfall <20 mm depending upon soil moisture and weather condition.  Keep soil moisture stable to avoid tuber deformation or cracking. Follow ridge and furrow method of irrigation according to soil moisture status. During irrigation apply water in furrow upto two third height of ridge should be submerged. Do not allow water to stand at the root of the potato. Proper drainage channel should be done.

advisory_title: Pest and Disease management (Common scab, Potato wart, Late blight, Aphids, Whitefly, Thrips, Leafhopper)

advisory_content: Continue irrigation every 8–10 days. Uniform moisture helps maximum tuber development. Avoid over-irrigation. Moisture stress should be avoided even if temperatures are lower; aim for uniform moisture throughout the root zone.

advisory_title: Pest and Disease management (Late blight, Common scab, Potato wart, Potato tuber moth)

advisory_content: Reduce irrigation frequency. Apply light irrigation only if wilting is observed. Stop irrigation 10–12 days before harvest to promote proper skin hardening and avoid tuber rotting.

advisory_title: Pest and Disease management (Late blight, Common scab, Potato wart, Potato tuber moth)

advisory_content: Do not allow water to stand at the root of the potato. Do proper drainage arrangements. Drying of vines helps in tuber hardening and easier harvest. In case of leaf blight infestation, mix Hexaconazole @ 2 grams in 1 liter of water and sprinkle. If root rot or blight occurs, remove the affected plants from the nursery and spray with Streptocycline @ 1 gm in 10 liters of water.

The main crop is ready for harvest within 75-85 days of planting depending upon the soil type and variety sown in our climatic condition of odisha. Cut the above ground part of the plant when it is ready to harvest i.e plant turns yellow brown. After 10-15 days harvest potato. Farmer should be very much careful while digging potato to avoid injuries in potato. Irrigation should be stopped 15 days before harvesting. Keep the harvested tubers in shed for 48 hours. All damaged and rotten tubers should be removed.

Curing of Tubers which helps heal minor cuts and reduces post-harvest diseases. Store for 7 to 10 days in well-ventilated shade or curing sheds of Temperature (15–20°C), Relative Humidity (85–90%) with proper air circulation. Cleaning and Grading by Remove soil, damaged, diseased, and green tubers. Grade tubers by size (small, medium, large) for uniform storage and marketing. Discard bruised or sprouted tubers to avoid rot spread.

Do Short-Term Storage (Seed Tubers or Fresh Market) like Well-ventilated cool rooms with temperature 10–15°C and Humidity 85–90% or heap storage. Cover heaps with straw and polythene during winter. For Cold Storage (Long-Term Storage) like Keep temperature of 2–4°C so as to Prevent sprouting of Seed Potatoes and 10–12°C to prevent sugar accumulation for processing potatoes and 7–10°C to Maintain texture and taste for Table Potatoes. Store in plastic crates or perforated gunny bags to enhance air circulation. Stack with space for air flow; use fans for ventilation in large cold stores. To control Dry rot, soft rot by Proper curing, avoid mechanical damage. Avoid light exposure during and after harvest.

Before Storage harvest during dry weather; avoid mechanical damage. Cure tubers well before storage. Remove infested tubers during grading. In Storage use well-ventilated, insect-proof stores. Keep store temperature below 18°C; ideal is 10–12°C. Apply insect-proof netting or plastic sheet lining in traditional heaps. Spread neem leaves or dry neem powder between layers of tubers in heaps. Fumigate with Aluminium Phosphide (2–3 tablets/ton) in airtight storage. Use Malathion 50% EC spray (0.05%) @  10 ml/ liter of water (to make 0.05% spray)~1 liter per 10 m² surface area on walls and floors of storage space before stacking tubers.

Rat cause serious damage like Gnawing of tubers, contamination with droppings and urine. They enter through holes in storage, broken walls or open ventilation. For management, Keep storage clean and dry. Close all rodent entry points using wire mesh or cement. Use rodent baiting stations with Zinc phosphide or Bromadiolone baits like Zinc Phosphide Bait (Zinc phosphide (2% bait formulation). Mix 25 g Zinc phosphide with 975 g cereal bait (wheat/ rice/ peanut cake) + a little oil (1–2 ml) to enhance palatability @ 10–15 g bait per bait station. Place in bait stations at 10–15 m intervals along walls, near burrows. Single dose, monitor for 2–3 days, replace if consumed, Bromadiolone Bait (Bromadiolone (0.005% ready-to-use bait) @ 10–15 g bait per bait point. In bait boxes or stations every 5–10 m in storage area. Daily replenishment until no consumption (usually 4–5 days). Place rat traps regularly; inspect weekly.

Clean store before stocking. Use insecticide spray (malathion 0.05%) on walls/floor. Keep all cracks sealed. Cure tubers 7–10 days to heal injuries. Remove injured/infected tubers. Maintain Cool conditions like temperature 10–12°C, dark, and dry with ventilation. Mix dry neem leaves/powder in layers of tubers. Fumigate the grains with Aluminium phosphide 56% @ 3 tablets per metric ton (MT) of stored potatoes.

Some important varieties for cultivation during pre-Kharif or Kharif season are Basant Bahar, LBG-17, PU-30, PU-19, Sarala, CO 6, Sashi, Kalinga urd 41 etc.

Farmers are advised to start land preparation immediately after the paddy harvest especially when grown under residual soil moisture after the harvest. Plough the field 2–3 times to break clods and obtain a fine tilth. Level the land properly to conserve residual moisture and ensure uniform germination. In areas with poor drainage, make broad beds and furrows or raised beds to avoid waterlogging. Apply well-decomposed FYM or compost @ 2 t/acre during the last ploughing to improve soil fertility and moisture-holding capacity. In case of dry soil, apply one pre-sowing irrigation to ensure uniform germination. Avoid deep sowing and ensure proper seed-soil contact. If soil moisture is inadequate, delay sowing until light rainfall or irrigation is available. Farmers should ensure proper field bunding to retain moisture and prevent runoff. In areas prone to moisture stress, apply light irrigation at flowering and pod formation stages, which are critical for yield improvement. Protect seedlings from pests like cutworm, termites.

Treat 8-10 kg of seeds are required for sowing in an acre area. Seed treatment should be done before 1 week of sowing with carbendazim @ 2 g/ kg or Carboxin 37.5 % + Thiram 37.5% D.S @ 2 -gram/ kg of seeds. On the day of sowing prepare a slurry of 20 g bacteria culture with jaggery @ 50 gram and 40 ml water for 1 kg seed and mix it with seeds, then shade dry and sow it in afternoon. Apply Trichoderma viride @ 4 -10 g/ kg of seed or Pseudomonas fluorescens @ 10 g/kg seed. To increase the biological nitrogen fixation, mix Rhizobium 200 g and PSB 250 g in 600 ml water per 10 kg of seed followed by drying for 12 hrs. in shade. Seeds are treated with Thiamethoxam 30% FS @ 10 ml/ kg or Imidachloprid 48% FS @ 5-10 ml/kg of seed. To prevent sucking pest and with carbosulfan 25 EC @ 2g/ Kg of seed to prevent nematode infestation. Sow the seeds at 20 cm x 10 cm spacing after seed treatment is done.
Plough the field till fine tilth and apply 4 kg urea, 35 kg DAP, 13 kg MOP along with 2 tons well decomposed cowdung manure. Sow the seeds at 25 c.m x 10 c.m spacing after seed treatment is done.
To manage weeds at early stages in these crops, spray pre-emergence herbicide Pendimethalin 30 % E.C @ 1 litre /acre at 1-2 DAS by mixing in 200-litre of water.

advisory_code: OD-ALL-K-GG-GS-Pest and disease (Bacterial Wilt, Stem fly, Dry Root rot, Aphids, Whitefly, Thrips)
advisory_title: Pest and disease (Bacterial Wilt, Stem fly, Dry Root rot, Aphids, Whitefly, Thrips)

advisory_content: If pre-emergence herbicide is not applied during sowing than to manage weeds in these crops post emergence application of herbicide like Imazethapyr 10 % SL(For both narrow and broadleaf weeds) @ 300-ml/acre or Quizalofop-Ethyl 5 % EC (Only for narrow weeds) @ 400-ml/acre should be applied at 15-DAS by mixing it in 200 litre of water. Always use flat-fan or flood-jet nozzle and clean water for herbicide spraying.

advisory_code:
OD-ALL-K-BG-VG-Pest management (Aphids, Thrips, Leafhopper, Whitefly, Tobacco caterpillar, Pod fly, Bihar Hairy Caterpillar)
Disease Management (Rust, Bacterial Wilt, Cercospora leaf spot, Anthracnose, Bacterial Leaf Spot / Blight, Powdery Mildew, Yellow Mosaic Virus, Dry Root rot)
advisory_title:
Pest management (Aphids, Thrips, Leafhopper, Whitefly, Tobacco caterpillar, Pod fly, Bihar Hairy Caterpillar)

advisory_content: During the flowering stage (30–45 DAS), Blackgram is highly sensitive to moisture stress, so maintaining adequate soil moisture is crucial. If there is no rainfall for 7–10 days, provide light irrigation (4–5 cm depth) using the furrow or check basin method, avoiding water stagnation. Ensure proper drainage to prevent diseases like powdery mildew and rust. Irrigate in the early morning or late afternoon to reduce evaporation losses. Mulching with dry straw or crop residues helps conserve soil moisture. Avoid over-irrigation, as it may cause flower drop. For better pod setting and uniform flowering, apply a foliar spray of 2% DAP solution or NAA @ 40 ppm during early flowering.

advisory_code:
OD-ALL-K-BG-F_Pest management (Aphids, Whitefly, Leafhopper, Pod borer, Tobacco caterpillar, Pod fly, Bihar Hairy Caterpillar)
Disease Management (Rust, Anthracnose, Bacterial Leaf Spot / Blight, Cercospora Leaf Spot, Powdery Mildew, Yellow Mosaic Virus, Anthracnose, Dry Root rot)
advisory_title:
Pest management (Aphids, Whitefly, Leafhopper, Pod borer, Tobacco caterpillar, Pod fly, Bihar Hairy Caterpillar)
Disease Management (Rust, Anthracnose, Bacterial Leaf Spot / Blight, Cercospora Leaf Spot, Powdery Mildew, Yellow Mosaic Virus, Anthracnose, Dry Root rot)

advisory_content: During the pod formation stage (43–56 DAS), maintaining adequate soil moisture is vital for proper pod development and grain filling in Blackgram. If there is no rainfall for 7–10 days, provide light to moderate irrigation (4–5 cm depth) using the furrow or basin method, avoiding waterlogging. Mulching with crop residues or straw helps conserve moisture and regulate soil temperature. Irrigate in the early morning or late afternoon to reduce evaporation losses and ensure good drainage. Monitor for pests like pod borer, aphids, whitefly, and tobacco caterpillar, which are active during this stage. Timely application of neem-based or recommended insecticides helps protect pods while maintaining optimal soil moisture.

advisory_code:
OD-ALL-K-BG-PF-Pest management (Pod borer, Bihar Hairy Caterpillar, Whitefly, Tobacco caterpillar, Pod fly)
Disease Management (Rust, Dry Root rot)
advisory_title:
Pest management (Pod borer, Bihar Hairy Caterpillar, Whitefly, Tobacco caterpillar, Pod fly)
Disease Management (Rust, Dry Root rot)

advisory_content: Harvest management and Storage practices in Blackgram. Pods should be harvested at 70-80 % maturity when most of the pods turn black. Threshing is done manually or using machineries like vertical flow pulse thresher cum winnower and sun dried for 3 - 4 days to attain a moisture content of 8-10%. Apply edible oil @200 ml/quintal of grain or neem oil @200-250 ml / 50 kg of grain and mix it well to store for longer period. Mix neem leaves or begonia leaves to prevent storage pest attack @ 50g/kg of seeds.

After harvesting Blackgram, dry the seeds until the optimum moisture content is attained. Clean the entire surface where the storage is to be done. Thereafter, mix 250 ml of any edible oil per quintal of seed to reduce the infestation of pest during the storage. Dried leaves of either Begonia or Neem @ 1 kg/Bag can also be kept inside the storage bag to avoid pest infestation. To decrease the pest infestation during storage, dip the gunny bags in boiling water for 15min then dry it properly under the sun before filling the seeds in the bags. Else prepare Malathion 5ml per litre of water solution and then dip the gunny bags in it for 15min and then dry it properly under the sun. Thereafter store the seeds in the bags.

Some important varieties for cultivation during Kharif season are OMH 14-27, HM 9, Vivek maize hybrid-27, HM 4, Vivek maize hybrid-17, P 3441, P 3501, P 3544, PMH 6, Bio 9544, CMH 08-287, CMH 08-292, DMH 121, LG 32.81 (Yuvaraj Gold),  HM 12, Seed Tech 2324, KDMH-017, KMH 3712, NK 30, NMH 920, NMH 803.

Land should be ploughed 3-4 times by cultivator to get fine tilth followed by land levelling. Apply 2 tonne of well decomposed FYM during final land preparation. For one acre of maize cultivation 6-7 kg of seeds is required

For one acre of maize cultivation 6-7 kg of seeds is required. Seeds should be treated before sowing to protect it from seed and soil borne diseases and pests. Apply Carboxin 37.5 % + Thiram 37.5% D.S @ 2 g / kg seed for leaf blight and Pythium Stalk Rot and Imidachloprid @ 4 g / kg or Fipronil @ 4 ml / Kg for termite and shoot fly. Mix Cyantraniliprole19.8% + Thiamethoxam 19.8% FS @ 4 ml / kg seed or Cyzapyr + Thiamethoxam @ 6 ml/ kg seed to control Fall Army Worm, Stemborer and aphids. Keep row to row spacing at 60 cm and plant to plant at 25 cm.  To maintain optimum plant population, use seed drill for sowing. Remove extra plants at 3 leaf stage (10 to 12 days after emergence) by keeping one plant per hill. Sowing is done at a depth of 5 cm.

Apply 48- kg of DAP, 40-kg of potash and 6 kg Urea per acre along with 2 tonnes of well decomposed FYM in furrows before sowing as basal fertilizer. Apart from this application of 10 kg Zinc sulphate, 4 kg boron and 12 kg Sulpher per acre enhances the crop yield.

The first weeding  should be done within 2-3 weeks after germination followed by second hoeing may be done at 6 to 7 weeks stage. Rotary peg weeder or power weeder can be used for more effective weeding. For chemical control of weeds in one acre, pre-emergence application of atrazine 50% WP @ 600-800 g or Pendimethalin 30%EC @600-900 g within 1-2 days after sowing effectively controls weeds up to one month.

Farmers should take care of irrigation during this time. If available, sprinkler irrigation up to the knee-height stage is optimum method for maize crop. In furrow irrigation method water should be given in furrow up to 2/3rd of the height of the ridge.

advisory_content: The grain can be harvested when cob-sheath turns brownish, grains become hard, and they do not contain more than 30- percent moisture in them. Black to brown spots appears on the cob when grains are removed. Tear off the sheath after cobs are removed from standing plants. Dry the cobs under the sun till the grains attains 12-15 % Moisture level. Use hand operated maize sheller or power operated maize sheller to remove the grains. After shelling, the grain can be dried for 2 to 3 days, cleaned and stored at 8 to 10 per cent moisture in gunny bag. Proper care should be taken to avoid damage due to insects and rodents.

Use the varieties suitable to Odisha condition like Kalinga groundnut 101, Devi, Kadri-9, TG-51, TG-38, CO-7, Dharani, OG-52-1 (Smruti), GPBD 4 (Vikas), TPG-41, TMV 14, Kalinga Groundnut -1 (Dhara), Kadri-6 (K-1240), ICGV-00350, JL-24, TAG-24 etc. For one acre 40-50 kg groundnut seed kernel is required.

The land is levelled by ploughing 3-4 times and the soil is levelled. Select well drained sandy loam soil, use improved plough (MB plough) and power tiller with rotavator or tractor with cultivator for good seed bed preparation. If there is waterlogging at the base of the tree, the yield of groundnut decreases. Therefore, slight slope should be made from one side to the other to drain the stagnant water from the land. During the last ploughing, 5 tons of cow dung is applied per hectare. If the soil is acidic, the nuts become nutless. Therefore, if the soil test shows acidity, Apply 5-6 cart load of well decomposed farmyard manure before each crop season. Use Chlorpyrifos 1.5% DP dust @ 10 kg/acre at final land preparation time in termite prone field.

For one acre 40-50 kg groundnut seed kernel is required. Treat the seeds with Carbendazim 50% WP @ 2-gram/ per kg of seeds or Carboxin 37.5 % + Thiram 37.5% D.S @ 3-gram/ kg or Thiamethoxam 75% SG @ 5 g/kg or Imidacloprid 48% FS @ 5 g/kg of seeds. After 7-days of this, treat the seeds with Rhizobium culture @ 25g per kg of seeds.

The seeds should always be sown in rows. The seeds should be sown at 25 cm between rows and 10 cm between plants. For this, 40-50 kg groundnut seed kernel is required. The soil is covered, and the seeds are sown on it. If seeds and fertilizers are sown in the field with the help of a seed drill, they can be sown at the right time, in a well-drained area. When sowing seeds with a plough disc, care should be taken to ensure that the seed is planted at a depth of 4 -6 cm. The seeds do not germinate if they are planted too deeply. After the seeds germinate, the soil should be covered with light manure.

Apply recommended basal fertiliser dose of 8: 16: 16 NPK/acre, therefore it is advisable to apply 35 kg DAP and 4-kg Urea and 26 kg MOP with Boron @ 6 kg per acre in the furrows before sowing. Apply well powdered Gypsum @ 100 kg/acre and incorporate into the soil along with the basal fertilisers, this will improve number of pods and pod filling. Mix the fertiliser with the soil so that seeds do not come in direct contact with the fertilizer. Keep line to line spacing of 25 cm and plant to plant spacing of 10 cm and put the seed in 3-4 cm depth. Micronutrient like should be used along with fertilizer application to reduce the pollen abortion and hollow heart disease of groundnut.

Apply pre-emergence herbicide Oxyfluorfen 23.5 % EC @ 80ml/acre or Pendimethalin 30 % EC @ 1-litre/acre or at 1-2 DAS. Always use flat-fan or flood-jet nozzle and clean water for herbicide spraying. Apply herbicide when soil is moist.

advisory_content: If pre-emergence herbicide is not applied during sowing than to manage weeds in Groundnut crop post emergence application of herbicide like Imazethapyr 10 % SL (For both narrow and broadleaf weeds) @ 300-ml/acre or Quizalofop-Ethyl 5 % EC (Only for narrow weeds) @ 400-ml/acre should be applied at 10-15 DAS by mixing it in 200 litres of water. Always use flat-fan or flood-jet nozzle and clean water for herbicide spraying.

Generally, all fertilizers are applied to peanut crops as basal fertilizer, and no fertilizer should be applied after flowering. Before flowering, do earthing up and remove weeds and grass. Doing this keeps the soil loose and allows water to penetrate the soil easily and form peg. For better flowering, use hormone Gibberellic acid @ 1 ml/liter or Tricontanol @ 1 ml/ liter of water and spray it on the plants twice, once at 30 days and again at 45 days after planting.

advisory_content: Irrigation Flowering Stage (25–35 Days After Sowing): At this stage, most plants begin to flower, and sufficient soil moisture is crucial. Moisture stress now will lead to poor flowering and reduced peg formation. Provide light irrigation when the top 5–7 cm soil becomes dry. Maintain even moisture throughout the root zone to encourage continuous flowering. Avoid over-irrigation or water stagnation, which can cause flower drop and fungal diseases. If possible, use drip or sprinkler irrigation for uniform moisture distribution.

advisory_title: Pest management (Red hairy caterpillar, Aphids, Thrips, Jassids, Leaf miner, Tobacco caterpillar, White grub, Termites)
Disease Management (Alternaria leaf blight, Bud necrosis, Dry Root rot, Early leaf spot )

advisory_content: Irrigation Peg Formation Stage (35–45 Days After Sowing) This stage is very critical because the fertilized flowers send pegs into the soil to form pods. Dry soil prevents peg penetration and reduces pod formation. Ensure adequate moisture in the top 10–15 cm soil layer. Apply a moderate irrigation at 7–10-day intervals depending on soil type. Avoid hard or crusted soil; light hoeing after irrigation helps aeration and peg entry. Waterlogging must be strictly avoided, as it may cause rotting of pegs.
